preFix.title: Composting cat and dog waste safely
preFix.subtitle: Using a dedicated in-ground digester to divert pet faeces from landfill
preFix.excerpt: In the UK, an estimated 1,000 tonnes of cat and dog waste goes to landfill every day. An in-ground pet waste digester uses natural soil bacteria to break down the faeces in place, diverting it from plastic bags in bins. The compost produced is not suitable for food crops but can be used around ornamental plants after a minimum of six months in the digester.
preFix.sourceNotes: WRAP pet waste management guidance. DEFRA Waste Framework Directive guidance on composting exemptions. WRAP Good Compost Guide section on pathogen destruction. RHS guidance on pet waste compost and food crop safety.

content.text: An
in-ground digester

is the simplest DIY approach to pet waste composting. A perforated plastic container (a repurposed bucket or a purpose-made unit) is buried so that its base is in contact with the soil. Waste is added from the top and broken down over time by soil bacteria and worms that enter through the base. The system is odour-free when correctly sited and managed, and avoids sending plastic poo bags to landfill.

attrs.title: This compost cannot go on vegetables

content.text: Pet waste compost from a cold digester retains
zoonotic pathogens
that are not destroyed at ambient temperatures. Never apply it to vegetable beds, fruit bushes, or any area where food crops grow. Use it only as an
ornamental-safe
amendment around shrubs, lawns, and trees, after a minimum of six months in the digester.
What you need
A digester unit: a purpose-made pet waste digester, or a 20-litre bucket with holes drilled into the lower half and the base cut out.
Septic activator powder: an enzyme and bacterial culture to start the digester. This is most useful in the first few weeks and in cold weather.

Dog or cat waste scooped without the plastic bag. If using biodegradable bags, only certified home-compostable (EN 13432 or equivalent) bags may be added. Standard biodegradable bags do not break down in cold composting conditions.

Site the digester at least 1 m away from any edible crop area, 3 m from a water feature or pond, and in a position children cannot access easily. Wash hands with soap for 20 seconds after adding waste or emptying the digester. Do not site above a known underground water supply or near a borehole.
Installation
Dig a hole slightly larger than the digester to a depth that buries the unit with only the lid above ground level. For a 300 mm diameter unit, this is typically 400 to 450 mm deep.
Drop the digester into the hole and backfill around it with the excavated soil, tamping gently to remove air pockets. The digester should be stable and not rock when the lid is opened.
Add the initial dose of
septic activator
according to the package instructions, then add the first batch of waste. After the first week, add activator once per month.
Add 30 ml of water after each deposit in dry weather to maintain the moisture level the bacteria need. In wet weather, no additional water is required.
Harvesting

After six months of use, remove the digester from the ground and set aside any partially decomposed waste in a labelled bag to continue maturing. Fork up the dark compost remaining at the base of the hole and from the lower portion of the digester. This is the
ornamental-safe compost
ready for use around shrubs or trees. Re-bury the digester in the original or a new location and continue the cycle.
What done well looks like

The digester lid stays odour-free when opened because the material is breaking down aerobically with adequate moisture. The waste level in the digester falls by 50 to 70 percent within three weeks of adding waste as decomposition proceeds. After six months a small yield of dark, earthy-smelling compost has accumulated at the base.
What done badly looks like

The digester was sited in a waterlogged clay corner of the garden. The holes in the base are blocked with clay, preventing drainage and preventing earthworm access. The unit is now a sealed container of anaerobic sludge with a strong smell each time the lid is opened. It was filled past the halfway mark before emptying, suppressing oxygen access to the lower material.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Add a double dose of septic activator and 100 ml of warm water to jump-start the microbial population. In winter, activity below 5 °C slows significantly — this is normal and will resume in spring.
attrs.cause: Cold soil temperature below 5 °C is slowing bacterial activity, or the holes in the unit are blocked with compacted soil
attrs.symptom: Waste not breaking down after three weeks

attrs.fix: Stop adding waste. Remove some material to reduce the fill level to two-thirds maximum. Add a cup of wood ash or sawdust to absorb excess moisture and raise the pH. If the soil is waterlogged, relocate the digester to a well-drained position.
attrs.cause: Anaerobic conditions because the unit is overfull, too wet, or in waterlogged soil

attrs.fix: Ensure the lid closes tightly and has a fly-proof seal. Temporarily add a 50 mm layer of wood ash or garden lime over the waste to deter flies and neutralise odour while the existing material breaks down.
attrs.cause: The digester was left open or the lid is not sealing correctly, allowing flies to lay eggs in the waste
attrs.symptom: Flies emerging from the digester when lid is opened

initialFindings.message: paragraph reads at grade 11.5 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
initialFindings.snippet: A digester unit: a purpose-made pet waste digester, or a 20-litre bucket with holes drilled into the lower half and the base cut out.
